title: Lent

infobox.value: * Omission of [Gloria](/wiki/Gloria_in_excelsis_Deo "Gloria in excelsis Deo") and [Alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia "Alleluia") * Veiling of religious images
* On [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") (Western) * On [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday") (Eastern)

* On [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday") ([Latin Catholic](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church")) * On [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") (Lutheran, Moravian, Anglican, and Western Orthodox) * On the Friday before [Lazarus Saturday](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday") (Eastern) * On [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter")
[Variable](#Date_and_duration) (follows the [paschal computus](/wiki/Date_of_Easter "Date of Easter"), and depends on denomination)
* 5 March – 17 April or 19 April (Western) * 3 March – 11 April (Eastern)
* 18 February – 2 April or 4 April (Western) * 23 February – 3 April (Eastern)
* 10 February – 25 March or 27 March (Western) * 15 March – 23 April (Eastern)
* 1 March – 13 April or 15 April (Western) * 28 February – 7 April (Eastern)

([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Quadragesima*,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) 'Fortieth') is the solemn [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") religious [observance](/wiki/Moveable_feast#Lent "Moveable feast") in the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") in preparation for [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"). It echoes the 40 days [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") spent fasting in the desert and enduring [temptation by Satan](/wiki/Temptation_of_Christ "Temptation of Christ"), according to the Gospels of [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke"), before beginning his [public ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus").[[2]](#cite_note-UMC_-_Lent-2)[[3]](#cite_note-The_Anglican_Catholic_Church-3) Lent is usually observed in the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Moravian](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), [United Protestant](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches") and [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") Christian traditions, among others.[[4]](#cite_note-Moravian2021-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Denominations_1-5)[[6]](#cite_note-EWTN-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Thomas2024-7) A number of [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism"), [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Reformed](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") (including certain [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Protestantism "Continental Reformed Protestantism"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") and [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church") churches), and [nondenominational Christian](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") churches also observe Lent, although many churches in these traditions do not.[[8]](#cite_note-Methodist2020-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Mennonite-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Christian observance before Easter

This article is about the general Christian period of repentance prior to Easter. For Lent in Eastern Christianity, see [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"). For other uses, see [Lent (disambiguation)](/wiki/Lent_(disambiguation) "Lent (disambiguation)").

**Lent** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Quadragesima*,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) 'Fortieth') is the solemn [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") religious [observance](/wiki/Moveable_feast#Lent "Moveable feast") in the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") in preparation for [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"). It echoes the 40 days [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") spent fasting in the desert and enduring [temptation by Satan](/wiki/Temptation_of_Christ "Temptation of Christ"), according to the Gospels of [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke"), before beginning his [public ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus").[[2]](#cite_note-UMC_-_Lent-2)[[3]](#cite_note-The_Anglican_Catholic_Church-3) Lent is usually observed in the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Moravian](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), [United Protestant](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches") and [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") Christian traditions, among others.[[4]](#cite_note-Moravian2021-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Denominations_1-5)[[6]](#cite_note-EWTN-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Thomas2024-7) A number of [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism"), [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Reformed](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") (including certain [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Protestantism "Continental Reformed Protestantism"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") and [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church") churches), and [nondenominational Christian](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") churches also observe Lent, although many churches in these traditions do not.[[8]](#cite_note-Methodist2020-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Mennonite-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Which days are enumerated as being part of Lent differs between [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") (see [below](#Date_and_duration)), although in all of them Lent is described as lasting for a total duration of 40 days, the number of days Jesus, as well as [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses") and [Elijah](/wiki/Elijah "Elijah"), went without food in their respective fasts.[[14]](#cite_note-Hynes1993-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Johnson2017-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) In Lent-observing [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") denominations, Lent begins on [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") and ends approximately six weeks later; depending on the [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") and local custom, Lent concludes either on the evening of [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday") (Holy Thursday),[[17]](#cite_note-17) or at sundown on [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") when the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil") is celebrated,[[18]](#cite_note-Langford_96-18) though in either case, Lenten fasting observances are maintained until the evening of Holy Saturday.[[19]](#cite_note-EWTN2-19) Being the [Lord's Day](/wiki/Lord%27s_Day "Lord's Day"), the Sundays of Lent are not regarded as days of fasting, though historically, abstinence from meat was enjoined on the Sundays of Lent (as with the other days of the Lenten season).[[20]](#cite_note-WJW-20) In [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") – including [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), [Eastern Catholics](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), [Eastern Lutherans](/wiki/Eastern_Lutheranism "Eastern Lutheranism"), and [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") – [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") is observed continuously without interruption for 40 days starting on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday") and ending on [Lazarus Saturday](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday") before [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[6]](#cite_note-EWTN-6)

Lent is a period of [repentance](/wiki/Repentance "Repentance"), which ends at the arrival of [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"). The purpose of Lent is the preparation of the believer for Easter through [prayer](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer"), [fasting](/wiki/Religious_fasting#Christianity "Religious fasting"), and [almsgiving](/wiki/Alms#Christianity "Alms") (the three pillars of Lent),[[22]](#cite_note-DLC2021-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Cumming2025-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Dougherty2016-24) as well as [mortifying the flesh](/wiki/Mortification_of_the_flesh "Mortification of the flesh"), [repentance](/wiki/Repentance_in_Christianity "Repentance in Christianity") of sins, [simple living](/wiki/Simple_living "Simple living"), and [self-denial](/wiki/Asceticism "Asceticism").[[25]](#cite_note-Jumper2020-25) In Lent, many Christians commit to fasting, as well as giving up certain luxuries in [imitation of Christ](/wiki/Imitation_of_Christ "Imitation of Christ")'s sacrifice during his [journey into the desert](/wiki/Life_of_Jesus#Baptism_and_temptation "Life of Jesus") for 40 days;[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-EB1911-27)[[28]](#cite_note-GassmannOldenburg2011-28) this is known as one's [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice").[[29]](#cite_note-29) Prior to the 6th century, Lent was normatively observed through the practice of the [Black Fast](/wiki/Black_Fast "Black Fast"), which enjoins fasting from food and liquids, with the allowance of one [vegetarian](/wiki/Christian_vegetarianism "Christian vegetarianism") meal after sunset.[[30]](#cite_note-Butler-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Kiernan-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Cléir2017-32) This form of fasting continues in certain denominations, such as the [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church").[[33]](#cite_note-Samaan2024-33) Many Lent-observing Christians also add a Lenten spiritual discipline, such as reading a [daily devotional](/wiki/Daily_devotional "Daily devotional") or praying through a [Lenten calendar](/wiki/Lenten_calendar "Lenten calendar"), to draw themselves near to [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity").[[34]](#cite_note-Crumm-34)[[35]](#cite_note-AmbroseCraig-Wild2007-35) Among Christians of the Roman Catholic, Evangelical Lutheran and Anglican denominations, the [Stations of the Cross](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross") are a devotion observed during Lent, especially on Fridays, in remembrance of the [Via Dolorosa](/wiki/Via_Dolorosa "Via Dolorosa") of Jesus.[[36]](#cite_note-Sheridan2014-36)

In most Lent-observing denominations, the last week of Lent is known as [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), which begins with [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday"). Following the New Testament narrative, Jesus' crucifixion is commemorated on Good Friday, and at the beginning of the next week the joyful celebration of Easter, the start of the [Easter season](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide"), which recalls the [Resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"). In some Christian denominations, Maundy Thursday, Good Friday, and Holy Saturday form the [Easter Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum").[[37]](#cite_note-37) The viewing of and participation in [Passion Plays](/wiki/Passion_Play "Passion Play") occurs during Lent, especially during Holy Week, which are often [interdenominational](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") productions.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Palms%C3%B6ndagsm%C3%A4ssa_i_Hedvig_Eleonora_kyrka.jpg/960px-Palms%C3%B6ndagsm%C3%A4ssa_i_Hedvig_Eleonora_kyrka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palms%C3%B6ndagsm%C3%A4ssa_i_Hedvig_Eleonora_kyrka.jpg)

An [Evangelical-Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") Mass at [Hedvig Eleonora Church](/wiki/Hedvig_Eleonora_Church "Hedvig Eleonora Church") on the sixth Sunday of Lent. Purple is the [liturgical colour](/wiki/Liturgical_colour "Liturgical colour") of the Lenten season.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Holy_Week_procession_in_Granada%2C_Nicaragua.jpg/330px-Holy_Week_procession_in_Granada%2C_Nicaragua.jpg)](/wiki/File:Holy_Week_procession_in_Granada,_Nicaragua.jpg)

Lent observers, including a [confraternity of penitents](/wiki/Confraternity_of_penitents "Confraternity of penitents"), carrying out a street procession during [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), in [Granada, Nicaragua](/wiki/Granada,_Nicaragua "Granada, Nicaragua"). The violet colour is often associated with penance and [detachment](/wiki/Detachment_(philosophy) "Detachment (philosophy)"). Similar Christian penitential practice is seen in other [Christian countries](/wiki/Christian_countries "Christian countries"), sometimes associated with fasting.[[39]](#cite_note-Knowlton2004-39)

The English word *Lent* is a shortened form of the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") word *lencten*, meaning "[spring season](/wiki/Spring_(season) "Spring (season)")", as its [Dutch language](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") cognate *[lente](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/lente#Dutch "wikt:lente")* ([Old Dutch](/wiki/Old_Dutch "Old Dutch") *lentin*)[[40]](#cite_note-40) still does today. A dated term in [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), *Lenz* ([Old High German](/wiki/Old_High_German "Old High German") *lenzo*), is also related. According to the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, 'the shorter form (? Old Germanic type \**laŋgito*- , \**laŋgiton*-) seems to be a derivative of \**laŋgo*- long […] and may possibly have reference to the lengthening of the days as characterizing the season of spring'. The origin of the -*en* element is less clear: it may simply be a [suffix](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix"), or *lencten* may originally have been a compound of \**laŋgo*- 'long' and an otherwise little-attested word \*-*tino*, meaning "day".[[27]](#cite_note-EB1911-27)

In languages spoken where Christianity was earlier established, such as [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") and [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), the term signifies the period dating from the 40th weekday before Easter. In modern Greek the term is Σαρακοστή (*Sarakostí*), derived from the earlier Τεσσαρακοστή (*Tessarakostí*), meaning "fortieth". The corresponding word in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), *quadragesima* ("fortieth"), is the origin of the terms used in Latin-derived languages and in some others.

Examples in the [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") group are: [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") *quaresma*, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") *carême*, [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") *coresma*, [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") *quaresima*, [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") *quaresma*, [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") *quaresma*, [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") *păresimi*, [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language") *caresima*, [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") *cuaresma*, and [Walloon](/wiki/Walloon_language "Walloon language") *cwareme*.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Examples in non-Latin-based languages are: [Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language") *kreshma*, [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language") *garizuma*, [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language") *korizma*, [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") and [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") *carghas*, [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language") *kwaresima*, [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_language "Filipino language") *kuwaresma*, and [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") *c(a)rawys*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In other languages, the name used refers to the activity associated with the season. Thus it is called "fasting period" in [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language") (*postní doba*), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") (*Fastenzeit*), and [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language") (*fasten*/*fastetid*), and it is called "The Great Fast" in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") (الصوم الكبير – *al-ṣawm al-kabīr*), [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") (ܨܘܡܐ ܪܒܐ *ṣawmā rabbā*), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") (*wielki post*), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") (великий пост – *vieliki post*), [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") (великий піст – *velyky pist*), and [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") (*nagyböjt*). [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), apart from a version based on the Latin term referring to the 40 days (see above), also has a "great fast" version: *postul mare*. [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") has three options, one of which means *fasting period*, and the other two referring to the 40-day period indicated in the Latin term: *vastentijd*, *veertigdagentijd* and *quadragesima*, respectively.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) In India, it is called चरम चालीसा (*Charam Chalisa* - meaning, "climax forty"). In [Maltese](/wiki/Maltese_language "Maltese language"), despite being a descendant of Arabic, the term *Randan* is used, which is distinctive when compared to the dialects of Arabic. Despite the centuries of [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") influences, the term remains unchanged since the [Arab occupation of Malta](/wiki/Aghlabids "Aghlabids").

## Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origin")]

The pattern of fasting and praying for 40 days is seen in the [Christian Bible](/wiki/Christian_Bible "Christian Bible"), on which basis the liturgical season of Lent was established.[[14]](#cite_note-Hynes1993-14)[[41]](#cite_note-Conte2001-41) In the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament"), the prophet [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses") went into the mountains for 40 days and 40 nights to pray and fast "without eating bread or drinking water" before receiving the [Ten Commandments](/wiki/Ten_Commandments "Ten Commandments") (cf. [Exodus 34:28](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Exodus%2034:28&version=nrsv)).[[41]](#cite_note-Conte2001-41) Likewise, the prophet [Elijah](/wiki/Elijah "Elijah") went into the mountains for 40 days and nights to fast and pray "until he reached Horeb, the mountain of God" when "the word of the Lord came to him" (cf. [1 Kings 19:8–9](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=1%20Kings%2019:8–9&version=nrsv)).[[41]](#cite_note-Conte2001-41) The early Christian bishop [Maximus of Turin](/wiki/Maximus_of_Turin "Maximus of Turin") wrote that as Elijah by "fasting continuously for a period of forty days and forty nights...merited to extinguish the prolonged and severe dryness of the whole world, doing so with a stream of rain and steeping the earth's dryness with the bounty of water from heaven", in the Christian tradition, this is interpreted as being "a figure of ourselves so that we, also fasting a total of forty days, might merit the spiritual rain of baptism...[and] a shower from heaven might pour down upon the dry earth of the whole world, and the abundant waters of the saving bath might saturate the lengthy drought of the Gentiles."[[15]](#cite_note-Johnson2017-15) In the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") went into the desert to fast and pray for 40 days and 40 nights; it was during this time that [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan") tried to [tempt him](/wiki/Temptation_of_Christ "Temptation of Christ") (cf. [Matthew 4:1–3](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Matthew%204:1–3&version=nrsv)).[[41]](#cite_note-Conte2001-41) The 40-day and night fasts of Moses, Elijah, and Jesus prepared them for their work.[[14]](#cite_note-Hynes1993-14)

[Early Christianity](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") records the tradition of fasting before Easter.[[42]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-42) For the meal of the day consumed after sunset (when the fast is broken), the [Apostolic Constitutions](/wiki/Apostolic_Constitutions "Apostolic Constitutions") permit the consumption of "bread, vegetables, salt and water, in Lent" with "flesh and wine being forbidden."[[42]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-42) The [Canons of Hippolytus](/wiki/Canons_of_Hippolytus "Canons of Hippolytus") authorize only bread and salt to be consumed during [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week").[[42]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-42) The practice of fasting and abstaining from alcohol, meat and [lacticinia](/wiki/Lacticinia "Lacticinia") during Lent thus became established in the Church.[[42]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-42)

In AD 339, [Athanasius of Alexandria](/wiki/Athanasius_of_Alexandria "Athanasius of Alexandria") wrote that the Lenten fast was a 40-day fast that "the entire world" observed.[[43]](#cite_note-Nampon1869-43) [Saint Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Saint_Augustine_of_Hippo "Saint Augustine of Hippo") (AD 354–AD 430) wrote that: "Our fast at any other time is voluntary; but during Lent, we [sin](/wiki/Christian_views_on_sin "Christian views on sin") if we do not fast."[[44]](#cite_note-Keenan1899-44) Church Father [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom") (A.D. 347–407) stated that the early Christians did not consume meat for the whole duration of Lent.[[42]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-42)

Three main prevailing theories exist on the finalization of Lent as a 40-day fast prior to the arrival of Easter Sunday: First, that it was created at the [Council of Nicea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") in 325 and there is no earlier incarnation. Second, that it is based on an [Egyptian Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_Egypt "Christianity in Egypt") post-[theophany](/wiki/Theophany "Theophany") fast. Third, a combination of origins [syncretized](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") around the Council of Nicea.[[45]](#cite_note-Russo2013-45) There are early references to periods of fasting prior to [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"). For instance, the *[Didache](/wiki/Didache "Didache")*, a 1st or 2nd-century Christian text, commends "the baptizer, the one to be baptized, and any others that are able" to fast to prepare for the sacrament.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

For centuries it has been common practice for baptisms to take place on Easter, and so such references were formerly taken to be references to a pre-Easter fast. [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian"), in his 3rd-century work *On Baptism*, indicates that Easter was a "most solemn day for baptism." However, he is one of only a handful of writers in the [ante-Nicene period](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_ante-Nicene_period "Christianity in the ante-Nicene period") who indicate this preference, and even he says that Easter was by no means the only favoured day for baptisms in his locale.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Since the 20th century, scholars have acknowledged that Easter was not the standard day for baptisms in the early church, and references to pre-baptismal periods of fasting were not necessarily connected with Easter. There were shorter periods of fasting observed in the pre-Nicene church ([Athanasius](/wiki/Athanasius "Athanasius") noted that the 4th-century Alexandrian church observed a period of fasting before Pascha [Easter]).[[45]](#cite_note-Russo2013-45) However it is known that the 40-day period of fasting – the season later named Lent – before [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide") was clarified at the Nicene Council.[[48]](#cite_note-48) In 363-64 AD, the [Council of Laodicea](/wiki/Council_of_Laodicea "Council of Laodicea") prescribed the Lenten fast as "of strict necessity".[[43]](#cite_note-Nampon1869-43)

## Date and duration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Date and duration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Lent_calendar.svg/250px-Lent_calendar.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lent_calendar.svg)

Some named days and day ranges around Lent and Easter in Western Christianity, with the fasting days of Lent numbered

The 40 days of Lent are calculated differently among the various [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") that observe it, depending on how the [date of Easter](/wiki/Date_of_Easter "Date of Easter") is calculated, but also on which days Lent is understood to begin and end, and on whether all the days of Lent are counted consecutively. Additionally, the date of Lent may depend on the calendar used by the particular church, such as the ([revised](/wiki/Revised_Julian_calendar "Revised Julian calendar")) [Julian](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") or [Gregorian calendars](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") typically used by Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, and [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") churches, or the [Ethiopian](/wiki/Ethiopian_calendar "Ethiopian calendar") and [Coptic calendars](/wiki/Coptic_calendar "Coptic calendar") traditionally used by some Oriental Orthodox churches.

### Roman Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Roman Catholic Church")]

Since 1970, in the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") Lent starts on Ash Wednesday and ends on the evening of [Holy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday") with the [Mass of the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Mass_of_the_Lord%27s_Supper "Mass of the Lord's Supper"). This comprises a period of 44 days. Historically, both the fasting and abstinence were required during the weekdays of Lent and with Sundays solely being days of abstinence;[[20]](#cite_note-WJW-20) the obligations of the Lenten fast continue through Good Friday and Holy Saturday, totaling 40 days (with the [Eucharistic Fast](/wiki/Eucharistic_Fast "Eucharistic Fast") applying as well).[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Although Lent formally ends on Holy Thursday, Lenten fasting practices continue until the Easter Vigil and additionally, the celebration of Easter is preceded by the Paschal fast.[[19]](#cite_note-EWTN2-19)[[51]](#cite_note-vatican.va-51)

In the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite"), Lent begins on the Sunday that follows what is celebrated as Ash Wednesday in the rest of the [Latin Catholic Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"), and ends as in the Roman Rite, thus being of 40 days, counting the Sundays but not Holy Thursday. The day for beginning the Lenten fast in the Ambrosian Rite is the Monday after Ash Wednesday. The special Ash Wednesday fast is transferred to the first Friday of the Ambrosian Lent. Until this rite was revised by Saint [Charles Borromeo](/wiki/Charles_Borromeo "Charles Borromeo"), the liturgy of the First Sunday of Lent was festive, celebrated in white [vestments](/wiki/Vestment "Vestment") with chanting of the [Gloria in Excelsis](/wiki/Gloria_in_Excelsis "Gloria in Excelsis") and [Alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia_(chant) "Alleluia (chant)"), in line with the recommendation in Matthew 6:16, "When you fast, do not look gloomy."[[52]](#cite_note-Thouret-52)[[53]](#cite_note-RC-en-lent-duration-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Initii-54)

During Lent, the Church discourages marriages, but couples may marry if they forgo the special blessings of the Nuptial Mass and limit social celebrations.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In the Catholic Church, the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council") directed that twofold character of Lent was to be "brought into greater prominence both in the [liturgy](/wiki/Catholic_liturgy "Catholic liturgy") and by liturgical [catechesis](/wiki/Catechesis "Catechesis")": Lent is a season for "recalling or preparing for baptism" and for penance, which "disposes the faithful, who more diligently hear the word of God and devote themselves to prayer, to celebrate the paschal mystery".[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The period of Lent observed in the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") corresponds to that in other churches of [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") that have similar traditions.

### Protestantism and Western Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Protestantism and Western Orthodoxy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/High_Altar_of_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church_during_Lent.jpg/250px-High_Altar_of_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church_during_Lent.jpg)](/wiki/File:High_Altar_of_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church_during_Lent.jpg)

In Western traditions, the [liturgical colour](/wiki/Liturgical_colour "Liturgical colour") of the season of Lent is violet. Altar crosses and religious statuary which show Christ in his glory are traditionally veiled during this period in the Christian year.

In Protestant and Western Orthodox Churches that celebrate it, the season of Lent lasts from [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") to the evening of [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday").[[6]](#cite_note-EWTN-6)[[57]](#cite_note-Luther_League_of_America-57) This calculation makes Lent last 46 days if the 6 Sundays are included, but only 40 days if they are excluded.[[58]](#cite_note-58) This definition is still that of the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"),[[59]](#cite_note-59) [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation"),[[60]](#cite_note-Northwestern_Publishing_House-60) [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"),[[61]](#cite_note-Kitch_130-61) [Methodist Church](/wiki/World_Methodist_Council "World Methodist Council"),[[18]](#cite_note-Langford_96-18) [Western Rite Orthodox Church](/wiki/Western-Rite_Orthodox_Churches "Western-Rite Orthodox Churches"),[[62]](#cite_note-Fenton-62) [United Protestant Churches](/wiki/United_Protestant_Church "United Protestant Church"),[[63]](#cite_note-63) and those of the [Reformed Churches](/wiki/Reformed_Church "Reformed Church") (i.e., [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), and [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist")) that observe Lent.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) (In the Episcopal Church, the main U.S. branch of the Anglican Communion, the 1979 Book of Common Prayer identifies Holy Week—comprising Palm/Passion Sunday through Holy Saturday—as a separate season after Lent;[[66]](#cite_note-66) but the Days of Special Devotion, to be observed by special acts of discipline and self-denial, include the weekdays of, but not the Sundays in, both Lent and Holy Week,[[67]](#cite_note-67) so the practical effect is the same as the traditional 40-day calculation.)

### Eastern Orthodoxy and Byzantine Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodoxy and Byzantine Rite")]

Main article: [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent")

In the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite"), i.e., the [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") (Greek: Μεγάλη Τεσσαρακοστή or Μεγάλη Νηστεία, meaning "Great 40 Days" and "Great Fast" respectively) is the most important fasting season in the church year.[[68]](#cite_note-68) In Eastern Orthodox circles, the season of Lent is known as "bright sadness" ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): χαρμολύπη, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek "Romanization of Greek"): *charmolypê*).[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The 40 days of Great Lent include Sundays, and begin on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"). The 40 days are immediately followed by what are considered distinct periods of fasting, [Lazarus Saturday](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday") and [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday"), which in turn are followed straightway by [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"). Great Lent is broken only after the Paschal (Easter) Divine Liturgy.

The Eastern Orthodox Church maintains the traditional Church's teaching on fasting. The rules for lenten fasting are the monastic rules. Fasting in the Orthodox Church is more than simply abstaining from certain foods. During the Great Lent Orthodox Faithful intensify their prayers and spiritual exercises, go to church services more often, study the Scriptures and the works of the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") in depth, limit their entertainment and spending and focus on charity and good works.

Some other churches that follow the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite"), including certain [Eastern Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic "Eastern Catholic") and [Eastern Lutheran](/wiki/Eastern_Lutheran "Eastern Lutheran") denominations have similar practices as those of Eastern Orthodoxy.
