Further information: [Fasting in the Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_of_the_Coptic_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Fasting and abstinence of the Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria")

Among the [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy"), there are various local traditions regarding Lent. Those using the [Alexandrian Rite](/wiki/Alexandrian_Rite "Alexandrian Rite"), *i.e.*, the [Coptic Orthodox](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria"), [Coptic Catholic](/wiki/Coptic_Catholic_Church "Coptic Catholic Church"), [Ethiopian Orthodox](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church"), [Ethiopian Catholic](/wiki/Ethiopian_Catholic_Church "Ethiopian Catholic Church"), [Eritrean Orthodox](/wiki/Eritrean_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo Church"), and [Eritrean Catholic](/wiki/Eritrean_Catholic_Church "Eritrean Catholic Church") Churches, observe eight continuous weeks of fasting constituting three distinct consecutive fasting periods:

1. a [Pre-Lenten](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent") fast in preparation for Great Lent
2. Great Lent itself
3. the Paschal fast during [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") which immediately follows Lent

As in the Eastern Orthodox Churches, the [date of Easter](/wiki/Computus "Computus") is typically reckoned according to the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"), and usually occurs later than Easter according to [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") used by Catholic and Protestant Churches.

### Ethiopian Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ethiopian Orthodoxy")]

Further information: [Fasting in the Ethiopian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church")

In Ethiopian Orthodoxy, fasting (*tsome*) lasts for 55 continuous days before Easter (*[Fasika](/wiki/Fasika "Fasika")*), although the fast is divided into three separate periods: Tsome Hirkal, the eight-day Fast of [Heraclius](/wiki/Heraclius "Heraclius"), commemorating the fast requested by the [Byzantine Emperor](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") [Heraclius](/wiki/Heraclius "Heraclius") before he reputedly set out to fight the [Sassanian Empire](/wiki/Sassanian_Empire "Sassanian Empire") and recover the [True Cross](/wiki/True_Cross "True Cross") which had been seized and taken from Jerusalem; Tsome Arba, 40 days of Lent; and Tsome Himamat, seven days commemorating [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week").[[70]](#cite_note-dw2017-03-22-70)[[71]](#cite_note-nenewe-71)[[72]](#cite_note-arega-72) Fasting involves abstention from animal products (meat, dairy, and eggs), and refraining from eating or drinking before 3:00 pm.[[70]](#cite_note-dw2017-03-22-70) Ethiopian devotees may also abstain from sexual activity and the consumption of alcohol.[[70]](#cite_note-dw2017-03-22-70)

### Quartodecimanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Quartodecimanism")]

[Quartodeciman](/wiki/Quartodeciman "Quartodeciman") Christians ended the fast of Lent on the [Paschal full moon](/wiki/Paschal_full_moon "Paschal full moon") of the [Hebrew calendar](/wiki/Hebrew_calendar "Hebrew calendar"), in order to celebrate the [Feast of Unleavened Bread](/wiki/Feast_of_Unleavened_Bread "Feast of Unleavened Bread") beginning on the 14th of [Nisan](/wiki/Nisan "Nisan"), whence the name derives. For this practice, they were excommunicated in the [Easter controversy](/wiki/Easter_controversy "Easter controversy") of the 2nd century A.D.

## Associated customs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Associated customs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Legionarios_en_la_procesi%C3%B3n_de_El_Encuentro_%28Semana_Santa_en_Ceuta%2C_2012%29.jpg/250px-Legionarios_en_la_procesi%C3%B3n_de_El_Encuentro_%28Semana_Santa_en_Ceuta%2C_2012%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Legionarios_en_la_procesi%C3%B3n_de_El_Encuentro_(Semana_Santa_en_Ceuta,_2012).jpg)

In many [Christian countries](/wiki/Christian_state "Christian state"), [religious processions](/wiki/Procession#Christian "Procession") during the season of Lent are often accompanied by a military escort both for security and parade. [Ceuta](/wiki/Ceuta "Ceuta"), Spain

Three traditional practices are to be taken up with renewed vigour during Lent; these are known as the three pillars of Lent:[[73]](#cite_note-Hilovsky2025-73)[[22]](#cite_note-DLC2021-22)

1. [prayer](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") ([justice](/wiki/Justice "Justice") toward God)
2. [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") (justice toward self)
3. [almsgiving](/wiki/Almsgiving#Christianity "Almsgiving") (justice toward neighbours)

Self-reflection, simplicity, and sincerity are emphasised during the Lenten season.[[25]](#cite_note-Jumper2020-25)

Often observed (especially on Fridays) in Christian churches of the Roman Catholic, Lutheran and Anglican denominations are the [Stations of the Cross](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross"), a devotional commemoration of [Christ's carrying the Cross](/wiki/Christ_Carrying_the_Cross "Christ Carrying the Cross") and [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus").[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) During Lent, Christian churches remove flowers from their [chancels](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") and veil [crucifixes](/wiki/Crucifix "Crucifix"), religious statues that show the triumphant Christ, and other elaborate religious symbols in violet fabrics. The [custom of veiling](/wiki/Lenten_shrouds "Lenten shrouds") is typically practised the last two weeks of Lent, beginning on the fifth Sunday of Lent (known as *Judica* Sunday or [Passion Sunday](/wiki/Passion_Sunday "Passion Sunday")) until [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"). During the Good Friday liturgy in Christian churches of the Catholic and Lutheran traditions, a wooden cross is venerated.[[76]](#cite_note-Gramenz2021-76) The same cross is often decorated with flowers on Easter Sunday.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

### Pre-Lenten observances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Pre-Lenten observances")]

Main articles: [Pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent") and [Shrovetide](/wiki/Shrovetide "Shrovetide")

During [pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent"), it is customary for Christians to ponder what [Lenten sacrifices](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice") they will make for Lent.[[78]](#cite_note-JKelvey2018-78)

The pre-Lenten period concludes with the opportunity for a last round of merrymaking, known as [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival"), [Shrovetide](/wiki/Shrovetide "Shrovetide"), or [Fastelavn](/wiki/Fastelavn "Fastelavn"), before the start of the sombre Lenten season. The traditions of carrying Shrovetide rods and consuming Shrovetide buns after attending church are celebrated.[[79]](#cite_note-ELCD2021-79)[[80]](#cite_note-Beadle1994-80)

On the final day of the season, [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday") or [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras"), many traditional Christians, such as [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics"), [Evangelical-Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans"), among others "make a special point of self-examination, of considering what wrongs they need to repent, and what amendments of life or areas of spiritual growth they especially need to ask God's help in dealing with."[[81]](#cite_note-Thaler2020-81)[[82]](#cite_note-Walker2011-82) As such, many churches (particulary those of the Catholic and Lutheran traditions) offer the sacrament of [Confession](/wiki/Confession "Confession") on Shrove Tuesday.[[83]](#cite_note-Lebanon2024-83)[[84]](#cite_note-Shepherd2021-84)[[85]](#cite_note-NWF2014-85) During Shrovetide, many churches place a basket in the [narthex](/wiki/Narthex "Narthex") to collect the previous year's [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") palm branches that were blessed and distributed during the [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") liturgies; on Shrove Tuesday, churches burn these [palms](/wiki/Palm_branch "Palm branch") to make the ashes used during the services held on the very next day, Ash Wednesday.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In historically [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") nations, Shrovetide is known as [Fastelavn](/wiki/Fastelavn "Fastelavn"). After attending the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy)#Lutheranism "Mass (liturgy)") on [Shrove Sunday](/wiki/Shrove_Sunday "Shrove Sunday"), congregants enjoy Shrovetide buns (fastelavnsboller), "round sweet buns that are covered with icing and filled with cream and/or jam."[[79]](#cite_note-ELCD2021-79) Children often dress up and collect money from people while singing.[[79]](#cite_note-ELCD2021-79) They also practise the tradition of hitting a barrel, which represents fighting Satan; after doing this, children enjoy the sweets inside the barrel.[[79]](#cite_note-ELCD2021-79) Lutheran Christians in these nations carry Shrovetide rods (fastelavnsris), which "branches decorated with sweets, little presents, etc., that are used to decorate the home or give to children."[[79]](#cite_note-ELCD2021-79)

In English-speaking countries such as the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), the day before Lent is known as Shrove Tuesday, which is derived from the word *shrive*, meaning "to administer the sacrament of [confession](/wiki/Confession_(religion) "Confession (religion)") to; to absolve."[[87]](#cite_note-Melitta_Weiss_Adamson,_Francine_Segan_2008-87) In these countries, [pancakes](/wiki/Pancakes "Pancakes") are associated with Shrove Tuesday because they are a way to use up rich foods such as eggs, milk, and sugar – rich foods which are not eaten during the season.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

The Carnival celebrations which in many cultures traditionally precede Lent are seen as a last opportunity for excess before Lent begins. Some of the most famous are the [Carnival of Barranquilla](/wiki/Carnival_of_Barranquilla "Carnival of Barranquilla"), the [Carnival of Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Carnival_of_Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Carnival of Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), the [Carnival of Venice](/wiki/Carnival_of_Venice "Carnival of Venice"), [Cologne Carnival](/wiki/Cologne_Carnival "Cologne Carnival"), the [New Orleans Mardi Gras](/wiki/New_Orleans_Mardi_Gras "New Orleans Mardi Gras"), the [Rio de Janeiro carnival](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_carnival "Rio de Janeiro carnival"), and the [Trinidad and Tobago Carnival](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago_Carnival "Trinidad and Tobago Carnival").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In stark contrast to traditions of merrymaking and feasting, Oriental Orthodox Churches practise a pre-Lenten fast in preparation for Lent which is immediately followed by the fast of Great Lent without interruption. One example is the traditional [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") festival of [Hano Qritho](/wiki/Hano_Qritho "Hano Qritho"), in which a feast is prepared from [bulgur](/wiki/Bulgur "Bulgur"), meat and eggs before the start of fasting.

### Fasting and Lenten sacrifice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Fasting and Lenten sacrifice")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Brooklyn_Museum_-_Jesus_Tempted_in_the_Wilderness_%28J%C3%A9sus_tent%C3%A9_dans_le_d%C3%A9sert%29_-_James_Tissot_-_overall.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Museum_-_Jesus_Tempted_in_the_Wilderness_%28J%C3%A9sus_tent%C3%A9_dans_le_d%C3%A9sert%29_-_James_Tissot_-_overall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_-_Jesus_Tempted_in_the_Wilderness_(J%C3%A9sus_tent%C3%A9_dans_le_d%C3%A9sert)_-_James_Tissot_-_overall.jpg)

*Jesus Tempted in the Wilderness (Jésus tenté dans le désert)*, [James Tissot](/wiki/James_Tissot "James Tissot"), [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum")

Main article: [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice")

See also: [Christian dietary laws](/wiki/Christian_dietary_laws "Christian dietary laws")

There are traditionally 40 days in Lent; these are marked by fasting, both from foods and festivities, and by other acts of [penance](/wiki/Penance "Penance"). Fasting is maintained for all 40 days of Lent (regardless of how they are enumerated; see [above](#Date_and_duration)). Historically, fasting and abstinence have been maintained continuously for the weekdays of the whole Lenten season, with Sundays being days of abstinence only.[[20]](#cite_note-WJW-20) The making of a [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice"), in which Christians give up a personal pleasure for the duration of 40 days, is a traditional practice during Lent.[[89]](#cite_note-Mortimer2016-89)

During [Shrovetide](/wiki/Shrovetide "Shrovetide") and especially on [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday"), the day before the start of the Lenten season, many Christians finalize their decision with respect to what [Lenten sacrifices](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice") they will make for Lent.[[78]](#cite_note-JKelvey2018-78) Examples include practising [vegetarianism](/wiki/Christian_vegetarianism "Christian vegetarianism") and [teetotalism](/wiki/Teetotalism "Teetotalism") during Lent as a Lenten sacrifice.[[90]](#cite_note-McDuff2013-90)[[91]](#cite_note-Bekhechi2017-91) While making a Lenten sacrifice, it is customary to [pray](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") for strength to keep it; many often wish others for doing so as well, e.g. "May God bless your Lenten sacrifice."[[92]](#cite_note-92) In addition, some believers add a regular spiritual discipline, to bring them closer to God, such as reading a Lenten [daily devotional](/wiki/Daily_devotional "Daily devotional").[[34]](#cite_note-Crumm-34)

For Catholics, Lutherans, Moravians, Anglicans, United Protestants, and Lent-observing Methodists and Reformed Christians, the Lenten penitential season ends after the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil") [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") or [Sunrise service](/wiki/Sunrise_service "Sunrise service"). Orthodox Christians also break their fast after the [Paschal Vigil](/wiki/Paschal_Vigil "Paschal Vigil"), a service which starts around 11:00 pm on Holy Saturday, and which includes the Paschal celebration of the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") of St. [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom"). At the end of the service, the priest blesses cheese, eggs, flesh meats, and other items that the faithful have been abstaining from for the duration of Great Lent.

Lenten traditions and liturgical practices are less common, less binding, and sometimes non-existent among some [liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") and [progressive Christians](/wiki/Progressive_Christianity "Progressive Christianity").[[93]](#cite_note-93) A greater emphasis on the anticipation of [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday") is often encouraged more than the penitence of Lent or Holy Week.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

Some Christians as well as [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") groups also interpret the Lenten fast in a positive tone, not as renunciation but as contributing to causes such as environmental stewardship and improvement of health.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) Even some [atheists](/wiki/Atheists "Atheists") find value in the Christian tradition and observe Lent.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

#### Lenten Black Fast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Lenten Black Fast")]

Historically, using the early Christian form known as the [Black Fast](/wiki/Black_Fast "Black Fast"), the observant does not consume food for a whole day until the evening, and at sunset, Christians traditionally break the Lenten fast of that day with a [vegetarian](/wiki/Christian_vegetarianism "Christian vegetarianism") supper (no food or drink is consumed in a day apart from that in the [Lenten supper](/wiki/Lenten_supper "Lenten supper")).[[32]](#cite_note-Cléir2017-32)[[98]](#cite_note-GuérangerFromage1912-98)[[30]](#cite_note-Butler-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Kiernan-31) In [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), many Christians continue this practice of fasting until sunset on [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") and [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), with many fasting in this manner throughout the whole season of Lent.[[99]](#cite_note-Addis2020-99)[[100]](#cite_note-Zaidi2022-100)

#### Daniel Fast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Daniel Fast")]

Christians of various traditions, including Catholics and Methodists, have voluntarily undertaken the [Daniel Fast](/wiki/Daniel_Fast "Daniel Fast") during the season of Lent, in which one abstains from "meat, fish, egg, dairy products, chocolates, ice creams, sugar, sweets, wine or any alcoholic beverages" (cf. [Daniel 10:3](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Daniel#10:3 "s:Bible (King James)/Daniel")).[[101]](#cite_note-RNS2013-101)[[102]](#cite_note-SMMRCC2021-102)[[103]](#cite_note-Hinton2016-103)

#### Lenten supper

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Lenten supper")]

After attending a worship service (often on Wednesday and Friday evenings), it is common for Christians of various denominations to conclude that day's Lenten fast together through a communal [Lenten supper](/wiki/Lenten_supper "Lenten supper"), which may be held in the church's [parish hall](/wiki/Parish_hall "Parish hall"). Lenten suppers ordinarily take place in the home setting during the 40 days of Lent during which a family (or individual) concludes that day's fast after a [mealtime prayer](/wiki/Grace_(prayer) "Grace (prayer)").[[104]](#cite_note-Lighthouse2018-104)

#### Abstinence from meat and animal products

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Abstinence from meat and animal products")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Linsen-M%C3%B6hren-Orangensuppe_%285417587993%29.jpg/250px-Linsen-M%C3%B6hren-Orangensuppe_%285417587993%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Linsen-M%C3%B6hren-Orangensuppe_(5417587993).jpg)

Lenten suppers often consist of a vegetarian soup, bread, and water in order to maintain the season's focus on abstinence, sacrifice, and simplicity.

Fasting has historically included abstinence from alcohol, meat, [lacticinia](/wiki/Lacticinia "Lacticinia") (dairy products), and other edible produce derived from animals (such as eggs), which has been enjoined continuously for the whole duration of the season including Sundays.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[42]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-42) Church Father [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom") stated that the early Christians did not consume meat for the whole duration of Lent.[[42]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-42) Throughout [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom"), some adherents continue to mark the season with a traditional abstention from the consumption of meat ([vegetarianism](/wiki/Christian_vegetarianism "Christian vegetarianism")), most notably among Catholics, Lutherans, and Anglicans.[[106]](#cite_note-ELCA1978-106)[[107]](#cite_note-Gavitt1991-107) The form of abstention may vary depending on what is customary; some abstain from meat for 40 days, some do so only on Fridays, or some only on Good Friday itself.[[106]](#cite_note-ELCA1978-106)[[33]](#cite_note-Samaan2024-33)

In Catholicism, lacticinia may be consumed by penitents in Spain and its colonised territories, per a pontifical decree of [Pope Alexander VI](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_VI "Pope Alexander VI"). Until 1741, meat and lacticinia were otherwise forbidden for the whole season of Lent, including Sundays. In that year, [Pope Benedict XIV](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XIV "Pope Benedict XIV") allowed for the consumption of meat and lacticinia during certain fasting days of Lent.[[108]](#cite_note-Thurston1904-108)

Abstinence from alcohol during the season of Lent has traditionally been enjoined "in remembrance of the Sacred Thirst of Our Lord on the Cross."[[42]](#cite_note-Kellner1908-42)[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Dispensations for the allowance of certain foods have been given throughout history, depending on the climate in that part of the world. For example, [Giraldus Cambrensis](/wiki/Giraldus_Cambrensis "Giraldus Cambrensis"), in his *Itinerary of [Archbishop Baldwin](/wiki/Baldwin_of_Forde "Baldwin of Forde") through Wales*, reports that "in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and the arctic regions", "great and religious persons" eat the tail of [beavers](/wiki/Beaver "Beaver") as "fish" because of its superficial resemblance to "both the taste and colour of fish." The animal was very abundant in Wales at the time.[[111]](#cite_note-111) [Saint Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Aquinas "Saint Thomas Aquinas") allowed for the consumption of sweetened foods during Lent, because "sugared spices", such as [comfits](/wiki/Comfits "Comfits"), were, in his opinion, digestive aids on par with medicine rather than food.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

Fasting practices are considerably relaxed in Western societies today, though in the Eastern Orthodox, [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox "Oriental Orthodox"), [Eastern Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic "Eastern Catholic"), and [Eastern Lutheran](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite_Lutheranism "Byzantine Rite Lutheranism") Churches abstinence from all animal products including eggs, fish, fowl, and milk is still commonly practised, so that, where this is observed, only vegetarian (or [vegan](/wiki/Vegan "Vegan")) meals are consumed for the whole of Lent, 48 days in the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite"). The [Ethiopian Orthodox Church's practices](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church") require a fasting period that is a great deal longer, and there is some dispute over whether fish consumption is permissible.

In the traditions of Lent-observing Western Christian churches, abstinence from eating some form of food (generally meat, but not dairy or fish products) is distinguished from fasting. In principle, abstinence is to be observed in Western Christianity on Ash Wednesday and on every Friday of the year that is not a [solemnity](/wiki/Solemnity "Solemnity") (a liturgical feast day of the highest rank); but in each country the [episcopal conference](/wiki/Episcopal_conference "Episcopal conference") can determine the form it is to take, perhaps replacing abstinence with other forms of penance.[[113]](#cite_note-canons-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)

#### Sexual abstinence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Sexual abstinence")]

The [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church"), a denomination of [Oriental Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Christianity "Oriental Orthodox Christianity"), teaches that during Lent believers "should refrain from physical relations to give themselves time for fasting and prayer ([1 Corinthians 7:5](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/1_Corinthians#7:5 "s:Bible (King James)/1 Corinthians"))."[[33]](#cite_note-Samaan2024-33) Also, the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") enjoins abstinence from sexual relations during Lent.[[116]](#cite_note-Menzel2014-116)

In Western Christianity, through the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), Christians abstained from sexual relations during the whole of Lent.[[117]](#cite_note-Brundage2009-117) In view of this, nine months after Lent, birth records were drastically low.[[118]](#cite_note-118) In Spain, according to researchers from the [University of Valencia](/wiki/University_of_Valencia "University of Valencia") and the [University of Alcalà](/wiki/University_of_Alcal%C3%A1 "University of Alcalá"), the custom of abstaining from sexual relations was widely practised until the end of the [Franco régime](/wiki/Franco_regime "Franco regime"), though some Western Christians voluntarily continue this practice today.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

### Specific fasting traditions by Christian denomination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Specific fasting traditions by Christian denomination")]

##### Catholicism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Catholicism")]

Further information: [Fasting in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Catholic_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Catholic Church")

Prior to 1966, the Catholic Church allowed Catholics of fasting age to eat only one full meal a day throughout all forty days of Lent, except on the Lord's Day. Catholics were allowed to take a smaller meal, called a [collation](/wiki/Collation_(meal) "Collation (meal)"), which was introduced after the 14th century A.D., and a cup of some beverage, accompanied by a little bread, in the morning.[[120]](#cite_note-120) The [1917 Code of Canon Law](/wiki/1917_Code_of_Canon_Law "1917 Code of Canon Law") allowed the full meal on a fasting day to be taken at any hour and to be supplemented by two collations, with the quantity and the quality of the food to be determined by local custom. Abstinence from meat was to be observed on Ash Wednesday and on Fridays and Saturdays in Lent.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

The Lenten fast ended on [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") at noon. Only those aged 21 to 59 were obliged to fast. As with all ecclesiastical laws, particular difficulties, such as strenuous work or illness, excused one from observance, and a dispensation from the law could be granted by a bishop or parish priest.[[122]](#cite_note-122) A rule of thumb is that the two collations should not add up to the equivalent of another full meal. Rather portions were to be: "sufficient to sustain strength, but not sufficient to satisfy hunger."[[123]](#cite_note-123)

In 1966, [Pope Paul VI](/wiki/Pope_Paul_VI "Pope Paul VI") reduced the obligatory fasting days from all forty days of Lent to Ash Wednesday and Good Friday, abstinence days to Fridays and Ash Wednesday, and allowed [episcopal conferences](/wiki/Episcopal_conference "Episcopal conference") to replace abstinence and fasting with other forms of penitence such as charity and piety, as declared and established in his [apostolic constitution](/wiki/Apostolic_constitution "Apostolic constitution") *[Paenitemini](/wiki/Paenitemini "Paenitemini")*; fasting on all forty days of Lent is still "strongly recommended", though not under pain of mortal sin.[[124]](#cite_note-124) This was done so that those in countries where the standard of living is lower can replace fasting with prayer, but "…where economic well-being is greater, so much more will the witness of asceticism have to be given…"[[125]](#cite_note-125)

This was made part of the [1983 Code of Canon Law](/wiki/1983_Code_of_Canon_Law "1983 Code of Canon Law"), which made obligatory fasting for those aged between 18 and 59, and abstinence for those aged 14 and upward.[[113]](#cite_note-canons-113) The [Irish Catholic Bishops' Conference](/wiki/Irish_Catholic_Bishops%27_Conference "Irish Catholic Bishops' Conference") decided to allow other forms of Friday penance to replace that of abstinence from meat, whether in Lent or outside Lent, suggesting alternatives such as abstaining from some other food, or from alcohol or smoking; making a special effort at participating in family prayer or in Mass; making the [Stations of the Cross](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross"); or helping the poor, sick, old, or lonely.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

The [Catholic Bishops' Conference of England and Wales](/wiki/Catholic_Bishops%27_Conference_of_England_and_Wales "Catholic Bishops' Conference of England and Wales") made a similar ruling in 1985[[127]](#cite_note-127) but decided in 2011 to restore the traditional year-round Friday abstinence from meat.[[128]](#cite_note-128) The [United States Conference of Catholic Bishops](/wiki/United_States_Conference_of_Catholic_Bishops "United States Conference of Catholic Bishops") has maintained the rule of abstention from meat on Friday only during Lent and considers poultry to be a type of meat but not fish or shellfish.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130)

The [Congregation of Mary Immaculate Queen](/wiki/Congregation_of_Mary_Immaculate_Queen "Congregation of Mary Immaculate Queen") (CMRI), a [Sedevacantist Catholic](/wiki/Sedevacantist_Catholic "Sedevacantist Catholic") congregation, requires fasting for its members on all of the forty days of the Christian season of repentance, Lent (except on the [Lord's Day](/wiki/Lord%27s_Day "Lord's Day")). The CMRI mandates under the pain of grave sin, abstinence from meat on Ash Wednesday, Good Friday, Holy Saturday and all Fridays of the year in general.[[131]](#cite_note-CMRI2018-131)

Even during Lent, the rule about solemnities holds, so that the obligation of Friday abstinence does not apply on 19 and 25 March when, as usually happens, the solemnities of [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Day#Catholic_traditions "Saint Joseph's Day") and the [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation") are celebrated on those dates. The same applies to [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day"), which is a solemnity in the whole of Ireland as well as in dioceses that have [Saint Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick "Saint Patrick") as their principal [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint"). In some other places, too, where there are strong Irish traditions within the Catholic community, a dispensation is granted for that day.[[132]](#cite_note-132) In [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), where Ash Wednesday often coincides with [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") celebrations, a dispensation is then granted from the laws of fast and abstinence, and the faithful are exhorted to use some other form of penance.[[133]](#cite_note-HK-133)

##### Lutheranism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Lutheranism")]

Following the birth of Lutheranism in the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), Lutheran church orders in the 16th century "retained the observation of the Lenten fast, and Lutherans have observed this season with a serene, earnest attitude."[[134]](#cite_note-Gassmann_180-134) Many [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") churches advocate fasting during Lent,[[28]](#cite_note-GassmannOldenburg2011-28)[[135]](#cite_note-135) especially on Ash Wednesday and Good Friday.[[136]](#cite_note-Hatch1978-136)[[28]](#cite_note-GassmannOldenburg2011-28)[[137]](#cite_note-Pfatteicher1990-137)[[138]](#cite_note-JacobsHaas1899-138) *A Handbook for the Discipline of Lent* published by the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America"), a [mainline](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") Lutheran denomination, offers a number of guidelines for fasting, abstinence, and other forms of self-denial during Lent:[[106]](#cite_note-ELCA1978-106)

> 1. Fast on Ash Wednesday and Good Friday with only one simple meal during the day, usually without meat.
> 2. Refrain from eating meat (bloody foods) on all Fridays in Lent, substituting fish for example.
> 3. Eliminate a food or food group for the entire season. Especially consider saving rich and fatty foods for Easter.
> 4. Consider not eating before receiving Communion in Lent.
> 5. Abstain from or limit a favorite activity (television, movies etc.) for the entire season, and spend more time in prayer, Bible study, and reading devotional material.
> 6. Don't just give up something that you have to give up for your doctor or diet anyway. Make your fast a voluntary self-denial (i.e. discipline) that you offer to God in prayer.[[106]](#cite_note-ELCA1978-106)

The [Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod](/wiki/Lutheran_Church%E2%80%93Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod"), a [confessional Lutheran](/wiki/Confessional_Lutheranism "Confessional Lutheranism") denomination, likewise encourages (but does not require) members to give things up for Lent, while emphasizing that the purpose of Lent is repentance from sin rather than minor acts of self-denial in themselves.[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140)

##### Moravianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Moravianism")]

Members of the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") voluntarily fast during the season of Lent, along with making a [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice") for the season as a form of penitence.[[141]](#cite_note-NHMC2021-141)

##### Reformed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Reformed")]

[John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), the principal figure in the development of Reformed theology, critiqued the practice of Lent in his [Institutes of the Christian Religion](/wiki/Institutes_of_the_Christian_Religion "Institutes of the Christian Religion") as a "superstitious observance," and observed that "Christ did not fast repeatedly (which he must have done had he meant to lay down a law for an anniversary fast), but once only, when preparing for the promulgation of the gospel."[[142]](#cite_note-142) Similarly, leading Reformed divines such as [Samuel Rutherford](/wiki/Samuel_Rutherford "Samuel Rutherford") rejected the obligation of Lent.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

The [Directory for Public Worship](/wiki/Directory_for_Public_Worship "Directory for Public Worship") produced by the [Westminster Assembly](/wiki/Westminster_Assembly "Westminster Assembly") in 1644 and approved by the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") in 1645 takes the position that "[t]here is no day commanded in scripture to be kept holy under the gospel but the Lord's day, which is the Christian Sabbath," and approves of fasting specifically "upon special emergent occasions" (cf. [days of humiliation and thanksgiving](/wiki/Days_of_humiliation_and_thanksgiving "Days of humiliation and thanksgiving")).[[144]](#cite_note-144) Accordingly, and in keeping with the Reformed [regulative principle of worship](/wiki/Regulative_principle_of_worship "Regulative principle of worship"), the Reformed churches have historically not observed Lent.[[145]](#cite_note-145)

Some churches in the Reformed tradition observe Lent today.[[27]](#cite_note-EB1911-27) For example, the [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America"), a Mainline Protestant denomination, describes the first day of Lent, [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), as a day "focused on prayer, fasting, and repentance," encouraging members to "observe a Holy Lent, by self-examination and penitence, by prayer and fasting, by practising works of love, and by reading and reflecting on God's Holy Word."[[146]](#cite_note-RCA2018-146)
Among Reformed Christians who do observe Lent, [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), which is towards the end of the Lenten season, is an important day of communal fasting, as it is for many Episcopalians, Lutherans, and Methodists.[[147]](#cite_note-RipleyDana1883-147)

##### Anglicanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Anglicanism")]

The *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* (1662) of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") designates "All the Fridays in the Year, except Christmas Day" as days of fasting and abstinence, alongside the forty days of Lent, the [Ember Days](/wiki/Ember_Days "Ember Days"), the [Rogation Days](/wiki/Rogation_Days "Rogation Days"), and the vigils of feast days.[[148]](#cite_note-Chisholm-148) [Saint Augustine's Prayer Book](/wiki/Saint_Augustine%27s_Prayer_Book "Saint Augustine's Prayer Book"), a companion to the *Book of Common Prayer*, states that fasting is "usually meaning not more than a light breakfast, one full meal, and one half meal, on the forty days of Lent."[[107]](#cite_note-Gavitt1991-107) It further states that "the major Fast Days of Ash Wednesday and Good Friday, as the American Prayer-Book indicates, are stricter in obligation, though not in observance, than the other Fast Days, and therefore should not be neglected except in cases of serious illness or other necessity of an absolute character."[[149]](#cite_note-149)

##### Methodism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Methodism")]

The historical Methodist homilies regarding the [Sermon on the Mount](/wiki/Sermon_on_the_Mount "Sermon on the Mount") stress the importance of the Lenten fast, which begins on Ash Wednesday.[[150]](#cite_note-AbrahamKirby2009-150) The [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") therefore states that:

> There is a strong biblical base for fasting, particularly during the 40 days of Lent leading to the celebration of Easter. Jesus, as part of his spiritual preparation, went into the wilderness and fasted 40 days and 40 nights, according to the Gospels.[[151]](#cite_note-UMCFasting2017-151)

[Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), which is towards the end of the Lenten season, is traditionally an important day of communal fasting for Methodists.[[147]](#cite_note-RipleyDana1883-147) Rev. Jacqui King, the minister of Nu Faith Community United Methodist Church in Houston explained the philosophy of fasting during Lent as "I'm not skipping a meal because in place of that meal I'm actually dining with God."[[152]](#cite_note-152)

The United Methodist Church teaches, in reference to one's [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice"), that "On each Lord's Day in Lent, while Lenten fasts continue, the reverent spirit of Lent is tempered with joyful anticipation of the Resurrection."[[153]](#cite_note-153)

The liturgical book *[The Sunday Service of the Methodists](/wiki/The_Sunday_Service_of_the_Methodists "The Sunday Service of the Methodists")* (put together by [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley")), as well as the Directions Given to Band Societies (25 December 1744), mandate fasting and abstinence from meat on all Fridays of the year (except Christmas Day, if it falls on a Friday).[[154]](#cite_note-Wesley1825-154)[[155]](#cite_note-McKnight2010-155)

### Other related fasting periods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Other related fasting periods")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Ashes_to_Go_at_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church.jpg/250px-Ashes_to_Go_at_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ashes_to_Go_at_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church.jpg)

The season of Lent begins on [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), most notably by the public imposition of ashes. In this photograph, a woman receives a cross of ashes on Ash Wednesday outside an Anglican church.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Ash_Wednesday_Mass_at_Nazareth_Evangelical_Lutheran_Church.jpg/250px-Ash_Wednesday_Mass_at_Nazareth_Evangelical_Lutheran_Church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ash_Wednesday_Mass_at_Nazareth_Evangelical_Lutheran_Church.jpg)

An Evangelical-Lutheran pastor distributes ashes during the celebration of [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") ([Divine Service](/wiki/Divine_Service_(Lutheran) "Divine Service (Lutheran)")) on Ash Wednesday.

The number 40 has many Biblical references:

* [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses") spent 40 days on [Mount Sinai](/wiki/Biblical_Mount_Sinai "Biblical Mount Sinai") with [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity")[[156]](#cite_note-156)
* [Elijah](/wiki/Elijah "Elijah") spent 40 days and nights walking to [Mount Horeb](/wiki/Mount_Horeb "Mount Horeb")[[157]](#cite_note-157)
* God sent 40 days and nights of rain in the great flood of [Noah](/wiki/Noah "Noah")[[158]](#cite_note-158)
* The Hebrew people wandered 40 years in the desert while traveling to the [Promised Land](/wiki/Promised_Land "Promised Land")[[159]](#cite_note-159)
* [Jonah](/wiki/Jonah "Jonah")'s prophecy of [judgment](/wiki/Judgment "Judgment") gave 40 days to the city of [Nineveh](/wiki/Nineveh "Nineveh") in which to repent or be destroyed[[160]](#cite_note-160)
* Jesus retreated into the wilderness, where He fasted for 40 days, and was [tempted](/wiki/Temptation_of_Christ "Temptation of Christ") by the [devil](/wiki/Devil_in_Christianity "Devil in Christianity").[[161]](#cite_note-161) He overcame all three of Satan's [temptations](/wiki/Temptation "Temptation") by citing scripture to the devil, at which point the devil left him, angels ministered to Jesus, and He began His [ministry](/wiki/Christian_ministry "Christian ministry"). Jesus further said that His disciples should fast "when the bridegroom shall be taken from them",[[162]](#cite_note-162) a reference to his Passion.
* It is the traditional belief that Jesus lay for 40 hours in the tomb,[[53]](#cite_note-RC-en-lent-duration-53) which led to the 40 hours of total fasting that preceded the Easter celebration in the [early Church](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity")[[163]](#cite_note-163) (the biblical reference to 'three days in the tomb' is understood by them as *spanning* three days, from Friday afternoon to early Sunday morning, rather than three 24-hour periods of time). Some Christian denominations, such as The Way International and Logos Apostolic Church of God,[[164]](#cite_note-164) as well as [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") scholar [E. W. Bullinger](/wiki/E._W._Bullinger "E. W. Bullinger") in *The Companion Bible*, believe Christ was in the grave for a total of 72 hours, reflecting the [type](/wiki/Typology_(theology) "Typology (theology)") of Jonah in the belly of the whale.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

One of the most important ceremonies at Easter is the [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") of the initiates on Easter Eve. The fast was initially undertaken by the [catechumens](/wiki/Catechumen "Catechumen") to prepare them for the reception of this [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament"). Later, the period of fasting from [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") until Easter Day was extended to six days, to correspond with the six weeks of training necessary to give the final instruction to those [converts](/wiki/Religious_conversion "Religious conversion") who were to be baptized.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Converts to Christianity](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity") followed a strict [catechumenate](/wiki/Catechumenate "Catechumenate") or period of instruction and discipline prior to receiving the [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament") of [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), sometimes lasting up to three years.[[166]](#cite_note-Hinson1981-166) In [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") near the close of the fourth century, classes were held throughout Lent for three hours each day. With the legalization of Christianity (by the [Edict of Milan](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan")) and its later imposition as the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), its character was endangered by the great influx of new members. In response, the Lenten fast and practices of [self-renunciation](/wiki/Asceticism#Christianity "Asceticism") were required annually of all Christians, both to show solidarity with the catechumens, and for their own spiritual benefit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Almsgiving

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Almsgiving")]

[Almsgiving](/wiki/Almsgiving#Christianity "Almsgiving") is one of the three pillars of Lent.[[23]](#cite_note-Cumming2025-23) The money that would normally go towards a luxury (given up as a [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice") during Lent), is donated at church as an [offering](/wiki/Offering_(Christianity) "Offering (Christianity)") towards helping the poor.[[167]](#cite_note-Vanspall2021-167)

### Prayer and devotion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Prayer and devotion")]

A common practice is the singing of the [Stabat Mater](/wiki/Stabat_Mater "Stabat Mater") hymn in designated groups. Among [Filipino Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_Philippines "Catholic Church in the Philippines"), the recitation of the epic of Christ' passion, called *[Pasiong Mahal](/wiki/Pasiong_Mahal "Pasiong Mahal")*, is also observed. In many Christian countries, grand religious processions and cultural customs are observed,[[168]](#cite_note-168) such as the [Stations of the Cross](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross").[[169]](#cite_note-169) A custom of [visiting seven churches](/wiki/Seven_Churches_Visitation "Seven Churches Visitation") during Holy Week to pray the Stations of the Cross and praying at each church, exists and has been done in an ecumenical context, involving Christians of the Catholic, Methodist, Episcopal and [Salvationist](/wiki/Salvation_Army "Salvation Army") traditions, among others.[[170]](#cite_note-170)

#### Omission of Gloria and Alleluia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Omission of Gloria and Alleluia")]

The *[Gloria in excelsis Deo](/wiki/Gloria_in_excelsis_Deo "Gloria in excelsis Deo")*, which is usually said or sung on Sundays at [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") (or Communion) of the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist"), and [Anglican rites](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), is omitted on the Sundays of Lent (as well as Sundays of Advent), but continues in use on [solemnities](/wiki/Solemnity "Solemnity") and [feasts](/wiki/Ranking_of_liturgical_days_in_the_Roman_Rite "Ranking of liturgical days in the Roman Rite") and on special celebrations of a more solemn kind.[[171]](#cite_note-Block2019-171)[[172]](#cite_note-172) Some [Mass compositions](/wiki/Mass_(music) "Mass (music)") were written especially for Lent, such as Michael Haydn's *[Missa tempore Quadragesimae](/wiki/Missa_tempore_Quadragesimae_(Michael_Haydn) "Missa tempore Quadragesimae (Michael Haydn)")*, without Gloria, in D minor, and for modest forces, only choir and organ. The Gloria is used on [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday"), to the accompaniment of bells, which then fall silent until the *Gloria in excelsis* of the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil").[[173]](#cite_note-173)

The Lutheran [Divine Service](/wiki/Divine_Service_(Lutheran) "Divine Service (Lutheran)"), the Roman Rite of the Catholic Church, the Anglican Churches, and the Presbyterian service of worship associate the *[Alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia "Alleluia")* with joy and omit it entirely throughout Lent,[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-Jr.2002-175) not only at Mass but also in the [canonical hours](/wiki/Canonical_hours "Canonical hours") and outside the liturgy. The word "Alleluia" at the beginning and end of the Acclamation Before the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel_(liturgy) "Gospel (liturgy)") at Mass is replaced by another phrase.

Before 1970, the omission began with [Septuagesima](/wiki/Septuagesima "Septuagesima"), and the whole Acclamation was omitted and was replaced by a [Tract](/wiki/Tract_(liturgy) "Tract (liturgy)"); and in the [Liturgy of the Hours](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours") the word "Alleluia", normally added to the *[Gloria Patri](/wiki/Gloria_Patri "Gloria Patri")* at the beginning of each Hour – now simply omitted during Lent – was replaced by the phrase *Laus tibi, Domine, rex aeternae gloriae (Praise to you, O Lord, king of eternal glory)*.

Until the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite") was revised by Saint [Charles Borromeo](/wiki/Charles_Borromeo "Charles Borromeo") the liturgy of the First Sunday of Lent was festive, celebrated with chanting of the Gloria and Alleluia, in line with the recommendation in Matthew 6:16, "When you fast, do not look gloomy."[[52]](#cite_note-Thouret-52)[[53]](#cite_note-RC-en-lent-duration-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Initii-54)

In the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite"), the Gloria ([Great Doxology](/wiki/Great_Doxology "Great Doxology")) continues to be used in its normal place in the [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins") service, and the Alleluia appears all the more frequently, replacing "God is the Lord" at Matins.

#### Veiling of religious images

A [crucifix](/wiki/Crucifix "Crucifix") on the high altar is veiled for Lent. Saint Martin's parish, [Württemberg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg "Württemberg"), Germany

In certain majority-[Christian states](/wiki/Christian_state "Christian state"), in which liturgical forms of Christianity predominate, religious objects were traditionally veiled for the entire 40 days of Lent. Though perhaps uncommon[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] in the United States of America,[*[relevant?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Stay_on_topic "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*] this practice is consistently observed in [Goa](/wiki/Goa,_India "Goa, India"), Malta, Peru, the Philippines (the latter only for the entire duration of Holy Week, with the exception of processional images), and in the Spanish cities: [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga"), and [Seville](/wiki/Seville "Seville"). In Ireland, before [Vatican II](/wiki/Vatican_II "Vatican II"), when impoverished rural Catholic convents and parishes could not afford purple fabrics, they resorted to either removing the statues altogether or, if too heavy or bothersome, turned the statues to face the wall. As is popular custom, the 14 [Stations of the Cross](/wiki/Stations_of_the_Cross "Stations of the Cross") plaques on the walls are not veiled.

Crosses were often adorned with jewels and gemstones, the form referred to as *Crux Gemmata*. To keep the faithful from adoring elaborately ornamented crucifixes, churches began veiling them in purple fabrics. The violet colour later came to symbolize penance and mourning.

Further liturgical changes in modernity reduced such observances to the last week of [Passiontide](/wiki/Passiontide "Passiontide"). In parishes that could afford only small quantities of violet fabrics, only the heads of the statues were veiled. If no violet fabrics could be afforded at all, then the religious statues and images were turned around facing the wall. Flowers were removed as a sign of solemn mourning.

In the [pre-1992 Methodist liturgy](/wiki/Book_of_Worship_for_Church_and_Home_(1965) "Book of Worship for Church and Home (1965)") and pre-1970 forms of the Roman Rite, the last two weeks of Lent are known as [Passiontide](/wiki/Passiontide "Passiontide"), a period beginning on the Fifth Sunday in Lent, which in the 1962 edition of the [Roman Missal](/wiki/Roman_Missal "Roman Missal") is called the First Sunday in Passiontide and in earlier editions Passion Sunday. All statues (and in England paintings as well) in the church were traditionally veiled in violet. This was seen as in keeping with John 8:46–59, the Gospel of that Sunday, in which Jesus "hid himself" from the people.

Within many churches in the United States of America, after the Second Vatican Council, the need to veil statues or crosses became increasingly irrelevant and was deemed unnecessary by some [diocesan](/wiki/Diocesan "Diocesan") bishops. As a result, the veils were removed at the singing of the [Gloria in Excelsis Deo](/wiki/Gloria_in_Excelsis_Deo "Gloria in Excelsis Deo") during the Easter Vigil. In 1970, the name "Passiontide" was dropped, although the last two weeks are markedly different from the rest of the season, and continuance of the tradition of veiling images is left to the discretion of a country's conference of bishops or even to individual parishes as pastors may wish.

On [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), the Anglican, Lutheran, and Methodist churches traditionally veiled "all pictures, statutes, and the cross are covered in mourning black", while "the chancel and altar coverings are replaced with black, and altar candles are extinguished." The fabrics are then "replaced with white on [sunrise](/wiki/Sunrise "Sunrise") on [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday")."[[176]](#cite_note-176)

### Vestments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Vestments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Chancel_on_Maundy_Thursday.jpg/250px-Chancel_on_Maundy_Thursday.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chancel_on_Maundy_Thursday.jpg)

The [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") of a [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") church decorated with red [paraments](/wiki/Parament "Parament"), the liturgical colour of the last week of Lent, [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), in the Lutheran and Anglican Churches[[177]](#cite_note-Gally1989-177)

In the Catholic, Lutheran, Methodist, and many Anglican churches, pastors and priests wear violet vestments during the season of Lent.[[178]](#cite_note-Escue2000-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179) Catholic priests and [deacons](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon") wear white vestments on the solemnities of [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph") and the [Feast of the Annunciation](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Annunciation "Feast of the Annunciation") (19 and 25 March). On the fourth Sunday of Lent, rose-coloured vestments may be worn, if available. Historically, black was also used for Lent: [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III") declared black to be the proper colour, though [Durandus of Saint-Pourçain](/wiki/Durandus_of_Saint-Pour%C3%A7ain "Durandus of Saint-Pourçain") asserted violet was preferable to black.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

In some Anglican churches, a type of unbleached linen or muslin known as "Lenten array" is worn during the first three weeks of Lent, crimson is worn during Passiontide, and on holy days, the colour proper to the day is worn.[[181]](#cite_note-181) In certain other Anglican churches, as an alternative to violet for all of Lent except Holy Week and red beginning on Palm Sunday through Holy Saturday, Lenten array, typically made of sackcloth such as burlap and trimmed with crimson cloth, often velvet, is worn, even during Holy Week—since the sackcloth represents penance and the crimson edges represent the Passion of Christ. Even the veils that cover the altar crosses or crucifixes and statuary (if any) are made of the same sackcloth with the crimson trim.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/5208-20080122-1255UTC--jerusalem-calvary.jpg/250px-5208-20080122-1255UTC--jerusalem-calvary.jpg)](/wiki/File:5208-20080122-1255UTC--jerusalem-calvary.jpg)

[Church of the Holy Sepulchre](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Sepulchre "Church of the Holy Sepulchre"), [Old Jerusalem](/wiki/Old_Jerusalem "Old Jerusalem") on [Golgotha](/wiki/Golgotha "Golgotha"), [Mount Calvary](/wiki/Mount_Calvary "Mount Calvary"), where tradition claims Jesus was [crucified](/wiki/Crucifixion "Crucifixion") and died

There are several holy days within the season of Lent:

* [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday") is the first day of Lent in [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity").
* [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") is the first day of Lent in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), such as the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") of the Catholic Church, the Lutheran Churches, and Methodist Churches. However, in the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite") and the [Mozarabic Rite](/wiki/Mozarabic_Rite "Mozarabic Rite"), there is no Ash Wednesday: Lent begins on the first Sunday and the fast begins on the first Monday.
* Lenten Sundays:
  + The first Sunday of Lent marks one of the weeks during which [Ember days](/wiki/Ember_days "Ember days") are observed in Western Christian churches.
  + The [second Sunday](/wiki/Second_Sunday_of_Lent "Second Sunday of Lent") is also known as the Sunday of Transfiguration or Reminiscere Sunday
  + The third Sunday was traditionally known as *Oculi Sunday*, reflecting the opening words of the [Introit](/wiki/Introit "Introit"), *Oculi mei semper ad Dominum* ("My eyes are ever toward the Lord"), from [Psalm 25](/wiki/Psalm_25 "Psalm 25"):15.[[182]](#cite_note-182)
  + The fourth Sunday in Lent, which marks the halfway point between Ash Wednesday and Easter Sunday, is referred to as [Laetare Sunday](/wiki/Laetare_Sunday "Laetare Sunday") by Catholics, Anglicans, and many other Christians, because of the traditional Entrance Antiphon of the Mass. Due to the more "joyful" character of the day (since *laetare* in Latin means rejoice), the priest and deacon have the option of wearing vestments of a rose colour (pink) instead of violet.
    - Additionally, the fourth Lenten Sunday, [Mothering Sunday](/wiki/Mothering_Sunday "Mothering Sunday"), which has become known as Mother's Day in the United Kingdom and an occasion for honouring mothers of children, has its origin in a 16th-century celebration of the [Mother Church](/wiki/Mother_Church "Mother Church").
    - The fourth Sunday of Lent has also been called "[Rose Sunday](/wiki/Rosalia_(festival)#Rose_Sundays "Rosalia (festival)")"; on this day the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") blesses the [Golden Rose](/wiki/Golden_Rose "Golden Rose"), a jewel in the shape of a rose.
  + The fifth Sunday in Lent, also known in some denominations as [Passion Sunday](/wiki/Passion_Sunday "Passion Sunday") (and in some denominations also applies to [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday")) marks the beginning of [Passiontide](/wiki/Passiontide "Passiontide").
  + The sixth Sunday in Lent, commonly called [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday"), marks the beginning of [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), the final week of Lent immediately preceding Easter.
  + The Sundays in Lent carry Latin names in German [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), derived from the beginning of the Sunday's [introit](/wiki/Introit "Introit"). The first is called Invocabit, the second Reminiscere, the third Oculi, the fourth Laetare, the fifth Judica, the sixth Palm Sunday.
* Wednesday of Holy Week, [Holy Wednesday](/wiki/Holy_Wednesday "Holy Wednesday") (also sometimes known as [Spy Wednesday](/wiki/Spy_Wednesday "Spy Wednesday")) commemorates [Judas Iscariot](/wiki/Judas_Iscariot "Judas Iscariot")'s bargain to betray Jesus.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184)[[185]](#cite_note-185)
* Thursday of Holy Week is known as [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday") or Holy Thursday, and is a day Christians commemorate the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") shared by Christ with his [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)").
* The next day is [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), which begins the Easter Triduum; on this day Christians remember Jesus' [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion "Crucifixion"), death, and burial.

### Easter Triduum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Easter Triduum")]

Main article: [Easter Triduum](/wiki/Easter_Triduum "Easter Triduum")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

In the Catholic, Anglican, Lutheran, Old Catholic, and many other traditions, the [Easter Triduum](/wiki/Easter_Triduum "Easter Triduum") is a three-day event that begins Maundy Thursday evening, with the entrance hymn of the Mass of the Lord's Supper. After this celebration, the consecrated [hosts](/wiki/Host_(liturgy) "Host (liturgy)") are taken solemnly from the altar to a place of reposition, where the faithful are invited to meditate in the presence of the consecrated Hosts. This is the Church's response to Jesus' question to the disciples sleeping in the [Garden of Gethsemane](/wiki/Gethsemane "Gethsemane"), "Could you not watch with me one hour?" On the next day, the liturgical commemoration of the Passion of Jesus Christ is celebrated at 3 pm, unless a later time is chosen due to work schedules.

This service consists of readings from the scriptures, especially [John the Evangelist](/wiki/John_the_Evangelist "John the Evangelist")'s account of the [Passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)") of Jesus, followed by prayers, veneration of the cross of Jesus, and a communion service at which the hosts consecrated at the evening Mass of the day before are distributed. The [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil") during the night between Holy Saturday afternoon and Easter Sunday morning starts with the blessing of a fire and a special candle, and with readings from Scripture associated with [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"). Then, the [Gloria in Excelsis Deo](/wiki/Gloria_in_Excelsis_Deo "Gloria in Excelsis Deo") is sung, water is blessed, baptism and [confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation "Confirmation") of adults may take place, the people are invited to renew the promises of their own baptism, and finally, Mass is celebrated in the usual way from the Preparation of the Gifts onwards.

Holy Week and the season of Lent, depending on [denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") and local [custom](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)"), end with Easter Vigil at sundown on Holy Saturday or on the morning of Easter Sunday. It is custom for some churches to hold sunrise services which include open air celebrations in some places.

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lent&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s [Radio Four](/wiki/Radio_Four "Radio Four") normally broadcasts during Lent a series of programmes called the *[Lent Talks](/wiki/Lent_Talks "Lent Talks")*.[[186]](#cite_note-186) These 15-minute programmes are normally broadcast on a Wednesday and have featured various speakers, such as [Christian apologist](/wiki/Christian_apologist "Christian apologist") [John Lennox](/wiki/John_Lennox "John Lennox").[[187]](#cite_note-187)

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), popular Catholic Prayer and Meditation App, Hallow, runs prayer events throughout Lent with the current, #Pray40 2025 edition having over 1,000,000 praying together through Lent.[[188]](#cite_note-188)
